reasoning: The Lean statement captures the informal claim precisely. In Mathlib, `Sylow p G` is defined as a maximal p-subgroup (via `IsPGroup`), matching the notion of "p-Sylow subgroup". `P.Normal` corresponds to "P ◁ G". The conclusion `∀ Q : Sylow p G, P = Q` directly expresses "P is the only p-Sylow subgroup". The primality hypothesis `hp : Nat.Prime p` mirrors the "p-Sylow" terminology in the informal statement. Notably, the statement holds for arbitrary (even infinite) groups via `IsPGroup.to_sup_of_normal_right`: if P is a normal p-subgroup and Q is any p-subgroup, then P ⊔ Q is a p-subgroup, and by maximality of both P and Q, we get P = Q. Hence no finiteness hypothesis needs to be added, and none is missing. The Lean formalization is logically equivalent to the informal statement.
